text.fullText: February 17, 2026 Lauren Kasparek Oceans, Wetlands, and Communities Division Office of Water Environmental Protection Agency 1200 Pennsylvania Avenue NW, Washington, DC 20460 Proposed Rule: Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations (Docket ID: EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929) Dear Administrator Zeldin: The Distribution Contractors Association (DCA) appreciates the opportunity to comment on the Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (NPRM) entitled “Updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations” (FR 91; No. 10; 2008; January 15, 2026). DCA represents contractors, suppliers and manufacturers who provide distribution construction services including installation, replacement and rehabilitation of natural gas distribution systems as well as gas transmission pipelines in communities across the country. The permitting process related to getting important energy projects off the ground has been an enduring issue facing the energy and construction industry for decades. Several lawmakers from both parties, alongside advocacy groups and industry stakeholders, have long expressed that lengthy regulatory reviews often delay critical projects and impede economic growth. While incremental progress has been made over the past several years, outdated permit requirements continue to hamper critical energy projects, especially those related to fossil fuels. However, following legislation passed in recent years, historic federal investments in American infrastructure have been made in a variety of markets (including renewable energy), and these investments have resulted in bipartisan efforts to get projects off the ground faster and in a more efficient way. DCA strongly supports EPA’s proposed rule to revise the Water Quality Certification Regulations. The proposal would restore Section 401 to its intended statutory purpose, provide greater regulatory certainty, and strengthen timely decision-making processes, all while maintaining robust water quality protections that are fundamental to the Clean Water Act (CWA). For many years, Section 401 implementation has faced significant challenges, including duplicative state and federal reviews, open-ended permitting delays, and the imposition of conditions unrelated to water quality. These issues have undermined the development of federally authorized infrastructure projects, often without delivering meaningful environmental improvements. The proposed revisions directly address these longstanding problems by clarifying the roles and responsibilities of certifying authorities and federal agencies, establishing clear timelines, and ensuring that conditions imposed are closely tied to water quality standards. DCA supports the general concepts of the NPRM, and especially provisions that would: • Clarify that Section 401 certification decisions and conditions must be limited to water quality requirements associated with discharges into waters of the United States. • Prohibit withdrawal-and-resubmission practices that circumvent the CWA’s one-year deadline for certification decisions. Such practices have caused multi-year delays for federally authorized projects, inability to plan construction sequencing and labor deployment, increase project costs and financing risk, all without any demonstrable improvement on water quality. • Establish uniform requirements for a complete certification request and to clearly define when the statutory review period begins. Streamlining the certification process will improve transparency, and the NPRM would help prevent unnecessary delays and reduce regulatory uncertainty for critical energy infrastructure projects. This, in turn, will support the continued growth and modernization of the nation’s energy network, promote economic development, and ensure that energy is delivered reliably and affordably to consumers across the United States. Furthermore, the proposed rule preserves the ability of states and tribes to protect water quality within their jurisdictions, consistent with the cooperative federalism principles that underpin the CWA. By reinforcing the statutory boundaries of Section 401, the proposal ensures that environmental protections remain strong while also fostering a more efficient and predictable permitting process. DCA appreciates EPA’s thoughtful approach in proposing these updates and urge the agency to finalize the rule as proposed. We stand ready to work with EPA, states, and other stakeholders to support effective implementation of the revised regulations and to advance both environmental and energy infrastructure goals. EPA’s proposed rule updating the Water Quality Certification Regulations represents a necessary and constructive step toward restoring lawful, predictable, and effective implementation of CWA Section 401. Clarifying the scope, enforcing statutory timelines, and standardizing procedures, the proposal would strengthen water quality protection while enabling timely delivery of essential energy infrastructure. We thank EPA for taking this important action to address longstanding problems associated with Section 401 of the CWA, and we thank you for your consideration.
